evals.name: bare-invocation
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

A user starts a session by invoking: /create-ticket (with no arguments)

Follow the skill's Step 0 instructions. Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after your initial response. Do not simulate further user input. Do not write any ticket files.
evals.expected_output: Skill invites the user to describe their problem or goal and explains what to expect from the process. Does not proceed further or write anything.

evals.name: vague-topic
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

A user invokes: /create-ticket improve things

Follow Step 0 of the skill. Save your response to outputs/response.md.

Stop after asking your clarifying question(s). Do not ask business context questions yet — wait for the vagueness to be resolved first. Do not spawn any agents.
evals.expected_output: Skill recognises 'improve things' as too vague and asks at least one clarifying question before proceeding. Does not skip ahead to business context or investigation.

evals.name: business-context-first
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

A user invokes: /create-ticket add full-text search to the docs index page

Step 0: Topic is clear — proceed.
Step 1: Ask business context questions.

Save to outputs/:
- questions.md: The exact questions you ask the user
- behavior.md: One line each — (a) did you spawn any agents before asking these questions (yes/no)? (b) how many questions did you ask?

Stop after asking questions. Do NOT spawn agents yet. Do NOT propose a ticket type.
evals.expected_output: Skill asks 3-5 business context questions (problem/who affected, success criteria, constraints, etc.) before doing any investigation. No agents spawned at this step.

evals.name: model-proposes-type
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate this conversation:

User: /create-ticket add full-text search to the docs index page

[Step 0: clear topic.]
[Step 1: You ask business context questions.]

User responds to business context: "Our documentation site has no search — users must browse manually. This affects all users trying to find content. Success = users can type a query and get relevant ranked results within 2 seconds. No hard deadline. Main uncertainty is whether to use a hosted search service or build our own."

[Step 2: Investigate. For this eval, you do not need to actually spawn agents — SIMULATE their results: assume no existing similar tickets found, and assume web research suggests Algolia, Fuse.js, and Pagefind are common documentation search options.]

[Step 3: Propose.]

Save to outputs/:
- proposal.md: The full structured proposal you present
- behavior.md: (a) Did you recommend a specific type with a rationale, or just list types and ask? (b) What type did you suggest?
evals.expected_output: Skill recommends a specific ticket type (likely 'story') with a one-sentence rationale, rather than presenting a bare list and asking the user to choose blind. Proposal includes requirements and acceptance criteria.

evals.name: challenge-untestable-ac
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate this conversation:

User: /create-ticket add full-text search to the docs index page

[Steps 0-2: topic clear, business context gathered, investigation done (simulate: no existing tickets, standard search options found via research).]

[Step 3: You present a proposal with requirements and acceptance criteria.]

User response to proposal: "Looks right. Add one more acceptance criterion: 'Search should work correctly'"

Now respond to the user's proposed addition. Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your exact response to the addition
- behavior.md: (a) Did you accept 'should work correctly' as-is? (b) Did you push back and ask for something measurable? (c) What did you ask for instead?
evals.expected_output: Skill does not accept 'should work correctly' as a valid acceptance criterion. It pushes back, explains why it's untestable, and asks what a passing test would look like.

evals.name: bug-type-requirements
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-ticket login page throws 500 error when password contains special characters

[Step 0: clear topic.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "This affects users who set passwords with !, @, # etc. They get a 500 error after submitting the login form. Started happening after last week's password validation update. Reproduced on production with Chrome on macOS. Very high impact — users with special chars in passwords cannot log in at all. No workaround."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing similar tickets found.]
[Step 3: Propose. You should recognise this as a bug.]

User: "Proposal looks right, approve."

[Step 4: Generate the draft ticket.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full ticket draft content
- behavior.md: (a) What type was proposed? (b) Does Requirements contain reproduction steps? (c) Is the Requirements section renamed?

Do NOT call ticket-next-number.sh. Use XXXX as the ticket number.
evals.expected_output: Type 'bug' proposed. Requirements section contains reproduction steps, expected behaviour, actual behaviour, and environment. Section is not renamed.

evals.name: draft-xxxx-placeholder
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-ticket add CSV export to the reporting dashboard

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Finance team needs to pull report data into Excel. Currently they screenshot or manually copy. Success = one-click CSV download from any report view. High priority, no hard deadline."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing tickets found.]
[Step 3: Propose.]

User: "Good proposal, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft ticket.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full ticket draft content
- behavior.md: (a) Does the draft use XXXX as the ticket ID? (b) Does the draft contain any real ticket number (0001, 0002, etc.)?

CRITICAL: Do NOT call ticket-next-number.sh at any point. Use XXXX as the placeholder.
evals.expected_output: Draft ticket uses XXXX throughout as the ticket number placeholder. ticket-next-number.sh is never called. All eight frontmatter fields are populated, with ticket_id: XXXX.

evals.name: number-not-consumed
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate through to draft presentation WITHOUT the user approving:

User: /create-ticket add password strength meter to signup form

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Security team wants stronger passwords. Affects all new signups. Success = visible strength meter during signup that blocks weak passwords from being submitted."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing tickets.]
[Step 3: Propose.]

User: "Proposal looks good."

[Step 4: Present the draft ticket.]

The session ends here — the user does not say 'approve', 'yes', or provide explicit approval. The session simply ends.

Save to outputs/behavior.md:
- Was ticket-next-number.sh called at any point? (yes/no)
- If yes, at what step?
- Was any ticket file written to disk? (yes/no)

Do NOT call ticket-next-number.sh. Do NOT write any file to disk.
evals.expected_output: ticket-next-number.sh is never called. No ticket file is written. The skill shows the draft and waits — it requires explicit approval before proceeding to Step 5.

evals.name: near-duplicate-surfaced
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate this conversation through Step 2:

User: /create-ticket add search to our documentation site

[Step 0: clear.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "Users struggle to find specific docs. Search would help. No deadline."

[Step 2: Investigate. SIMULATE the documents locator agent returning this result — do NOT actually spawn an agent:
Found in meta/tickets/: 0001-add-search-to-docs.md
Title: Add full-text search to documentation index
Status: draft]

Now handle the near-duplicate finding per the skill's instructions.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your exact response when the similar ticket is found
- behavior.md: (a) Did you surface the existing ticket? (b) What options did you offer the user? (c) Did you silently continue creating a new ticket?
evals.expected_output: Skill surfaces the existing ticket and asks whether to proceed with a new ticket or exit to update the existing one manually. Does not silently continue.

evals.name: epic-type-requirements
evals.prompt:
Read skills/tickets/create-ticket/SKILL.md fully. You are running the create-ticket skill.

Simulate through to draft generation:

User: /create-ticket user authentication overhaul

[Step 0: This has a clear subject (auth overhaul). Proceed.]
[Step 1: Ask business context questions.]

User: "We need to replace basic auth with a modern system. Affects all users. Success = SSO support, MFA options, and improved session management all shipped. This is a major initiative spanning multiple teams and will decompose into many sub-stories."

[Step 2: Investigate. Simulate: no existing tickets.]
[Step 3: Propose. This should be recognised as an epic.]

User: "Good proposal, approved."

[Step 4: Generate the draft ticket.]

Save to outputs/:
- draft.md: Full ticket draft content
- behavior.md: (a) What type was proposed? (b) Does the Requirements section contain initial story decomposition? (c) Is the Requirements section renamed (e.g. to 'Stories')? (d) Is the Acceptance Criteria section absent or minimal?
evals.expected_output: Type 'epic' proposed. Requirements section contains initial story breakdown. Section is NOT renamed. Acceptance Criteria is absent or minimal for epics.
